item.title: This project lets you play rock, paper, scissors versus your Raspberry Pi, complete with hand gesture recognition
item.itunes:title: This project lets you play rock, paper, scissors versus your Raspberry Pi, complete with hand gesture recognition
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • This Raspberry Pi project uses AI to play rock, paper, scissors, with over 95% accuracy and low latency.
  • No need for external server - AI processing on Raspberry Pi leads to quicker results and cost savings.
  • Possibilities extend beyond the game: tech could assist those with accessibility needs, and practical applications await.

The best Raspberry Pi projects are a lot of fun to use, and they teach you something while you build them. If you've ever wondered about using AI to identify hand gestures but are unsure where to get started, why not challenge it to a game of rock, paper, scissors? Not only do you learn how to build an AI system that can detect what hand gesture you're throwing, but you can put your SBC in its place and beat it at a game or two. It knows what it did.

 Related
7 creative uses for Raspberry Pi you probably haven’t tried yet

Your Raspberry Pi is good for more than just a media center or retro game emulator

5

You, too, can play rock, paper, scissors against your Raspberry Pi 5

In a post on Hackster, tinkerer Citrus posted their new rock, paper, scissors-based SBC project. It uses the Raspberry Pi 5, a Raspberry Pi Touch Display, and the Raspberry Pi AI camera to make a system that can detect what hand sign you're making.

However, Citrus didn't want the Raspberry Pi to take what's on the screen and beam it over to an AI model on an external server to work out what's happening. Not only does it take a while for the results to come back, but it'd likely cost a fortune to process. The solution? Use all the AI processing hardware on the Raspberry Pi to churn through the data and get a response quicker at no additional cost.

The above video shows the game in action. It seems to be both speedy and accurate when detecting what hand sign you're throwing. Sure enough, Citrus confirms that the system works very well:

Experimental results show that the model performs excellently, with gesture classification accuracy and recall consistently above 95%. Furthermore, the system demonstrates robust performance across a wide range of environmental conditions, with extremely low latency and efficient real-time processing power, suitable for practical interactive applications such as the rock-paper-scissors game demonstrated. This work provides practical and innovative solutions for low-cost, efficient and robust hand gesture recognition for commercial applications in interactive entertainment, healthcare and smart home systems

While this is a fun project by itself, there are lots of ways you could apply this tech other than playing rock, paper, scissors. For instance, you could allow people with accessibility needs to control technology with a single hand gesture. Here's hoping people find cool ways to put this tech to use.

item.title: This cool 3D-printed rubber band-powered cartridge is perfect for miniature firing ranges
item.itunes:title: This cool 3D-printed rubber band-powered cartridge is perfect for miniature firing ranges
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • These 3D-printed rubber band cartridges can shoot projectiles with high velocity.
  • Use caution with these cartridges, as they can be dangerous and are not suitable for aiming at people.
  • Consider using the cartridges for target practice on inanimate objects rather than in competitive scenarios.

Whenever humanity invents a new thing, people get to work on figuring out how to use it to make it fling objects at really high speeds. I haven't really seen people achieve this goal with 3D printing, but today is the day that changes. Someone posted a rubber band-powered cartridge you can use to shoot little pellets around, but I wouldn't recommend firing it at people.

 Related
This Raspberry Pi project creates a floating image using a 150-year-old technique, and I love it

Oldie but a goodie.

5

This rubber band-powered cartridge can fire a mean shot

In a post on the 3D printing subreddit, user IdonthaveQuestions posted a video of their 3D printed cartridges. Loading them is easy; add a rubber band, put a pellet on it, push it down with a loader like it's a 21st-century musket, then turn it 90 degrees. It'll then keep the pellet in a primed position, which then fires when you tap on its underside hard enough.

So, the first thing you may think of when looking at this project is making these the perfect weapon in your ongoing projectile war with your rivals. However, it's best you don't actually point this thing at another living being, as they're more powerful than they look. In fact, the creator added a disclaimer that's mostly about not taking out an eyeball:

  • It's a prototype and it might be unstable.
  • Make sure there's no kids around. they might swallow the projectile.
  • DO NOT look into the hole of the loaded cartridge.
  • This Thing Hurts.
  • When loading, Twisting counter-clockwise must be done well. (It might launch itself)
  • DO NOT give it a imapact [sic], It might launch itself

As such, you'll be better off firing these little darts at inanimate objects without anyone else around. Maybe line up some cans, or maybe even 3D print some targets to ping things off of.

If you want to make your own, check out the schematics over on Maker World. The creator is also working on a gun that will fire these pellets, so keep an eye out for that, too.

item.title: This self-hosted Discord music bot looks like Spotify, and I'll never use anything else
item.itunes:title: This self-hosted Discord music bot looks like Spotify, and I'll never use anything else
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Discord has long been the default hangout spot for online communities, gamers, and even friends. Whether you’re gaming with friends or just vibing on a programming channel like me, bots add a lot of value to the experience. Music bots, especially, have been a key part of Discord culture for years, letting users queue up tracks from YouTube, SoundCloud, or even local files. But in a world where most music bots are either basic by design or get shut down due to API crackdowns, Vocard stands out. It’s not just another music bot. It’s a fully self-hosted experience with a slick Spotify or Music Assistant-style dashboard that adds a touch of professionalism to your Discord server.

Since I set it up, there's been no going back. Vocard brought that perfect balance of aesthetics, functionality, and control. It’s open source, runs entirely on your own infrastructure, and the dashboard feels like something Discord would ship.
4 reasons why I'm ditching Discord to self-host Mumble — and you should too

It's time to leave Discord and host your own voice servers again.

5

Setting up Vocard

Not plug-and-play, but worth the effort

Getting started with Vocard is a little more involved than your average Discord bot. This isn’t a simple invite-and-play bot like some of the other existing options. You need to self-host it, which means spinning up your own server or running it locally. The project is split into the core Vocard bot and the separate Vocard-Dashboard, which gives you that stunning web UI for an overarching view of the music selection and more.

Installation involves setting up a Lavalink server for audio streaming, configuring environment variables, and deploying both the bot and dashboard via Docker. It’s not hard if you’re familiar with Docker and Node.js, but it’s definitely not beginner-friendly. I struggled with it. That said, the documentation is solid, and the GitHub repos are actively maintained.

Once everything is running, the basics are pretty straightforward. You get a fully integrated web player that syncs with your Discord server. Users can control music playback, search for tracks, and browse playlists using a clean and modern interface. It works on desktop and mobile browsers and feels fast and responsive with an extremely polished experience.

A polished experience

Feels like Spotify inside Discord

What really blew me away was how good the Vocard dashboard looks and feels. Most self-hosted projects look like side projects cobbled together with minimal effort made towards the user experience. Vocard looks like a polished commercial product. The dark theme, the layout, and the controls all echo Spotify in the best way possible. You have a full queue, player controls, volume settings, and even metadata for each track.

The bot supports YouTube, SoundCloud, and direct links to build a queue from different sources. It handles long playlists well, offers snappy search, and displays album art when available. But it’s not just about playback. Vocard is also incredibly responsive to Discord commands and integrates tightly with voice channels. I observed that it handled reconnects gracefully, and the playback doesn’t stutter even when switching channels. The bot interface inside Discord is minimal and clean. You can queue songs through the chat, but the best experience is through the dashboard.

Why Vocard stands out

Total control and zero compromises

What sets Vocard apart isn’t just its looks. It’s the fact that you’re in full control of the stack. No third-party servers, no data collection, no surprise shutdowns because of API restrictions. You’re hosting it, you’re managing it, and you’re the one in charge. That matters a lot when dealing with music streaming and unexpected shutdowns.

Vocard also scales quite well. Though I tested it with a very small group of friends, you can run it for a larger group of friends or an even larger community with hundreds of users. As long as your server can handle the load, the bot performs flawlessly. Updates are regular, and the community offers help and suggestions on Discord, which is promising.

Another huge plus is customization. Since you’re hosting the dashboard, you can tweak the theme, change the branding, or even fork the UI to suit your server’s vibe. Though it's beyond my capabilities, if you want to integrate it with other tools or bots, you can. The flexibility here is unmatched.

The only music bot I need

I’ve used a lot of music bots over the years. Some were easy to set up but lacked polish. Others had great features but felt clunky in day-to-day use. Vocard hits that sweet spot. It looks beautiful, works reliably and gives you total control over your music setup on Discord.

If you’re willing to spend a little time on the initial setup, this bot rewards you with an experience that rivals commercial music services. It’s open source, actively maintained, and offers a Spotify-style UI that’s incredibly fun to use. I can’t see myself switching to anything else anytime soon, and once you try it, you’ll probably feel the same.

item.title: 5 rarest PlayStation 1 games you may never own
item.itunes:title: 5 rarest PlayStation 1 games you may never own
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
The PlayStation 1 is one of the greatest video game consoles ever released. To many, the console has a library of games that people view as iconic classics and phenomenal experiences that are great to go back to and play again. As one of the first platforms to put a big focus on 3D visuals for games, a generation of players have very fond memories of the original PlayStation. Along the way, as the console garnered a legacy of classic releases, some PlayStation games became rare and very elusive to collectors.

One can find a lot of the beloved PlayStation games that people always bring up. It's not difficult to pick up original copies of the classics that made the console what it is viewed as today. However, some games for the PlayStation have managed to become valuable over the years due to their scarcity and history. These games are very uncommon to anyone who loves the console, and are not as easily obtained as any other collectible. These are 5 of the rarest PlayStation 1 games you may never get to own, and that's okay. Not everybody will get to have these gems within their collection.

 Related
The 5 rarest PlayStation 2 games you may never own

These PlayStation 2 games are so rare and expensive that you may never get a chance to own them. Here's why they're so elusive!

20

5 The Misadventures of Tron Bonne

A legendary spin-off

Source: Capcom

Fans of the Mega Man series know all about Tron Bonne and her spin-off title for the PlayStation. It's a part of the franchise that Capcom hardly revisits, for one reason or another. But on the PlayStation back in 1999, Capcom released The Misadventures of Tron Bonne as a prequel to the first Mega Man Legends game. What made this title special was not just because it was a spin-off from a beloved series, but also the support it had for the Pocket Station in Japan, which the North American version did not. The game is an action-adventure where you control the charismatic Tron Bonne, undertaking tasks in three different gameplay styles that are inspired by the Mega Man Legends titles. Tron interacts with her Server Bots, a character that has appeared occasionally in other Capcom titles.

A loose copy of The Misadventures of Tron Bonne is valued at $250, which is pretty steep for just the disc. But complete in a box with everything, the price of the game can skyrocket to $500. Some auctions of this game have been known to reach as high as $1,500 when sold. Not bad for an obscure spin-off based on a beloved franchise. The game had a limited worldwide release, making it a good gem for Mega Man fans to collect.

4 Rayman 2: The Great Escape (Watch Bundle edition)

Time to pay up for this classic

Rayman 2: The Great Escape is another game that came out in 1999 and was released by Ubisoft. Although it was available for the Nintendo 64 and Sega Dreamcast, the PlayStation version of the game is pretty popular. All three versions of the game have some slight differences, with a few hidden areas in the PlayStation version being removed. However, they all play fundamentally the same. Rayman 2 is a 3D platformer that has players gathering lums and traversing obstacles over the course of multiple areas. The Rayman series is known for having a colorful cast of goofy characters with wild personalities, and Rayman 2: The Great Escape is no exception. The title hero has limbs that float around his body, allowing him to do all sorts of interesting things to fight enemies and move around the environment.

Rayman 2: The Great Escape on the original PlayStation had a bundle that came with a clear-colored watch. This version is a bit more expensive than the standard release. Loose without its contents, the game can be priced at around $290 alone. But in a complete package with everything included, this bundle can be valued at anywhere between $500 and $1,300 in total.

3 Mortal Kombat 3 (Jewel Case version)

The limited kombat case

Mortal Kombat 3 is a very divisive game for fans of the franchise. To some, it fundamentally changed the series to be faster and more furious with its fighting, but to others, it went too far in a number of ways. Regardless of how one might feel, Mortal Kombat 3 was a high point for the series back in 1995 for multiple platforms. However, the PlayStation 1 version of the game was touted by series creator Ed Boon to be the one that was as close to arcade-perfect as possible for home consoles. Unfortunately, technical issues within the PlayStation game, such as loading mid-match for Shang Tsung transformations, became a problem for fans. As a whole, the game didn't receive as big of a reception compared to its predecessors.

The release of Mortal Kombat 3 was pretty big, but there was a variation of the game that was limited. Most copies of the game were printed in the large format case for PlayStation releases, with a small batch of units given the jewel case later on. The smaller jewel case is the more valuable version of the game, which can garner a price of $500 loose. But for anyone with a copy that is complete in the box and in good condition, the value of it can be much higher. Some auctions of a Mortal Kombat 3 jewel case have sold for $900 on the lower end, but as much as $2,300 on the higher end of things.

2 Syphon Filter 3 (Original Version)

Before the world changed

Source: Sony Computer Entertainment

When it comes to stealth action games, the PlayStation has no shortage of them. Some titles in the genre went on to become iconic classics, while others became cult hits that PlayStation gamers fondly remember. Syphon Filter 3 is one of those cult hits from 1999, which put players in the role of a special ops soldier undergoing missions to stop international terrorists from causing havoc. The gameplay was in third-person and involved a lot of shooting and stealth to take down enemies and complete missions. Unfortunately, the original release for Syphon Filter 3 was scheduled for a time right after the 9-11 terrorist attack on the World Trade Center, as well as during an anthrax scare in the United States. This led to changes being made to the box art for the game, as well as a delayed release to November of that same year.

Despite the changes to the game's packaging, some copies with the original Syphon Filter box art managed to get out of stores despite a recall. This original box art makes the game more valuable. A loose copy of Syphon Filter 3 can fetch around $575 for anyone, but a complete-on-box copy of the game is valued at around $1,000 in total. A few auctions for a complete copy have even managed to sell as high as $2,600 at the time of sale. There's a lot of history tied to this PlayStation game, but not all of it comes from its gameplay and legendary title.

1 NFL Blitz 2000 (Greatest Hits edition)

A blitz that cost a fortune

If there's one thing that people love, it's sports games that are exciting to play. And just like what NBA JAM did for NBA basketball, NFL Blitz did the same for NFL football. But on the PlayStation 1 in 1999, a sequel to NFL Blitz made its way to the console. It's the third game in the Blitz series, which has updated team rosters and a few minor balance changes to its gameplay. NFL Blitz 2000 is like other arcade sports titles of the era, taking different aspects of the game of football and hyper-exaggerating them to cartoonish heights. The deep passes, the powerful tackles, and the ridiculous plays are all part of the charm that makes this football game stand apart from popular simulation football games like Madden NFL Football. Although NFL Blitz 2000 didn't add a lot of new original content compared to its predecessors, the game was still received fairly well by critics and fans.

The Greatest Hits release of NFL Blitz 2000 is unique because it was never supposed to have one. This version of the game was recalled at one point before launching, but some copies managed to get out into the wild. A regular copy of NFL Blitz 2000 is very cheap and easy to find, but the Greatest Hits version is far from that. A loose copy of this version of the game can be worth over $1,700 to collectors, with a complete-in-box copy going for as much as $5,200. The reason the prices are so high is because of how few copies are known to be around. A few instances of the Greatest Hits version of NFL Blitz 2000 have been known to sell for as much as $13,500 at auction, which sounds absolutely nuts. However, with so few copies of the game circulating, PlayStation and football collectors find this to be very valuable.

You are not ready to pay

Many great PlayStation 1 titles have been released, but not all of them demand the same value among collectors. Some games are just so scarce that they have become incredibly valuable gems of the console that are not easily obtained. And for some, it's a good enough reward to chase these games down to complete a collection. For others, however, they may never get to own any of these rare original PlayStation games. Unless you're ready to drop some serious cash to get them, you might be better off admiring these PlayStation 1 gems from a distance. And chances are, very few will ever be ready to do so.

 Related
The rarest Sega Dreamcast games you'll probably never own

These Sega Dreamcast games are so rare and expensive, you may never get to own them. Here's why!!!

2

item.title: No Nintendo Switch 2 Camera? No problem; just use your iPhone instead
item.itunes:title: No Nintendo Switch 2 Camera? No problem; just use your iPhone instead
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • Nintendo Switch 2 allows third-party hardware like an iPhone to function as a webcam.
  • YouTuber "Will It Work?" demonstrated the setup using USB-C to HDMI cables.
  • Recycling old phones into webcams for the Switch 2 is a cost-effective alternative.

Nintendo has been weirdly accepting of third-party hardware with the Switch 2. We learned a little while ago that third-party mice work just fine on the console, which was a little surprising given how the company could lock people into using Nintendo-produced hardware. Now, it seems that the same hardware freedom has been granted to third-party webcams, too, to the point where you can set up an iPhone to act as your console's camera.

 Related
I've spent a day with my Nintendo Switch 2, and I'm in love already

Nintendo's new generation seems to be a homerun

Someone found a way to use their iPhone as their Nintendo Switch 2 webcam

As spotted by Engadget, this cool feat was performed by the YouTuber "Will It Work?". As you might imagine from their channel name, they dedicated a video to see if they could get an iPhone's camera to work as a Nintendo Switch 2 webcam.

To achieve this, they grabbed an inexpensive USB-C to HDMI cable, plugged the USB-C end into the iPhone, plugged the HDMI side into a USB capture cable, then plugged the end of that cable into the Switch. They then grabbed an app that lets you see your camera without any UI cluttering the screen, as the Switch 2 will grab whatever it "sees" on your phone as the webcam feed.

Sure enough, once Will It Work booted up the app and got everything plugged in, the Nintendo Switch 2 recognised the iPhone as a webcam. Now, they can use all of the cool features that come with using a webcam with their Switch 2 for just the price of a few cables and a spare (or even current) phone. Granted, it doesn't look like a Piranha Plant, but it's still a great way to recycle old technology.

If you like the idea of giving your outdated, unused phones a second life as a camera, check out our resources on the topic:

  • I turned my old smartphone into a webcam for my PC — here's how it went
  • Here’s how to turn your Android phone into a wireless webcam for PC and laptop
  • Here's how I turned my PC into a viewfinder for my phone's camera
  • Camo app for Android turns your phone into a better webcam for your PC

item.title: One of our favorite OLED gaming monitors just dropped to an all-time low price
item.itunes:title: One of our favorite OLED gaming monitors just dropped to an all-time low price

Response Time
0.03ms

One of our favorite gaming monitors is down to its best price as it drops to a record low at just $629. You get a fantastic OLED panel and impressive performance. This is a deal you don't want to pass up.

$629 at Amazon

There are plenty of great monitors you can check out if you're looking to buy something new. But if you want to grab one of our favorites, we think this Asus ROG Swift PG27AQDM monitor is going to be a fantastic option. You get impressive colors that pop, deep black levels, and impressive performance numbers.

 Related
Asus ROG Swift PG27AQDM review: For those who crave a gorgeous OLED and high refresh rates for gaming

Among the best OLEDs you can buy.

Best of all, it's come down in price since our review, with a hefty discount that drops it down to its lowest price yet at $629. And if that discount wasn't enough, you can now also score a free copy of DOOM: The Dark Ages, which we recently reviewed and loved, praising its amazing combat and art direction.

What's great about the Asus ROG Swift PG27AQDM gaming monitor?

Despite it being a little bit older, the Asus ROG Swift PG27AQDM is still a great monitor to go with if you're on a smaller budget. When it comes to the specifications, you're getting a vibrant 26.5-inch OLED panel that delivers accurate colors with 99% DCI-P3 color gamut coverage and a peak brightness of 1,000 nits.

As mentioned before, you also get excellent performance numbers as well, with the monitor featuring a 240Hz refresh rate and 0.03ms response time. You also get support for Nvidia's G-Sync technology, which should keep things looking buttery smooth, even in the most demanding and fast-paced games.

Of course, with this kind of power, you also need excellent cooling abilities, which is where this monitor's custom heatsink comes in. While the design of the monitor is fairly simple, you also get small pops of flair as well, thanks to its RGB lighting that can be customized.

And if you're someone that relies on their monitor for everything, you'll be happy to know that connectivity is also pretty good as well, with two HDMI and one DisplayPort, along with a 3.5mm audio jack and USB ports. We couldn't find much to complain about with this monitor, with the price being one of our top concerns.

And with this discount, that problem has been solved, making it a fantastic monitor to pick up if you've been looking for something new. So get it while you can from Amazon, or check out some of our other favorite monitors if this one isn't quite what you're looking for.

item.title: 4 quirky and fun ways I repurpose my old NAS
item.itunes:title: 4 quirky and fun ways I repurpose my old NAS
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Whether it’s a pre-built server or a rig you put together yourself, a Network-Attached Storage solution tends to last for years – even a decade or two – if you take proper care of it. That said, there are times when you might be inclined to upgrade your NAS. Perhaps your pre-built enclosure doesn’t feature enough drive bays to meet your storage needs. Or maybe your ancient HDD-only NAS doesn’t have the necessary slots to accommodate blazing-fast NVMe drives for high-speed file transfers and backup operations.

Once you upgrade to a faster storage server, your older NAS will probably end up gathering dust. Since I have a habit of purchasing spare hardware, I’ve come up with a couple of projects to repurpose old NAS units and add them to my ever-growing home lab.

 Related
Building your own NAS? These 5 operating systems are the best

If you prefer building your own NAS from scratch, here are five operating systems you need to check out

6

4 Long-term data archival unit

Preferably an offsite backup rig

Although NAS units are designed to be used 24/7 for file transfers and backups, you could just as easily use them as a data archival machine. A secondary NAS that I no longer use for my everyday tasks is the perfect guinea pig for this project, as it can hold my precious files for years. I prefer to use my HDD-only NAS units for long-term storage over all-flash enclosures.

SSDs use electrical charges to store data inside NAND flash cells, which can dissipate over the span of a year (maybe two if you're lucky) – making SSDs terrible for long-term (or cold) storage. Personally, I stash my data archival NAS in my PC closet, but you can take this project to the next level by keeping it in a remote location and accessing it over a Tailscale (or Headscale/WireGuard for self-hosting lovers) network.

3 Game storage server

For my ROM files and Steam games

Considering that HDDs can result in long loading times in modern titles, accessing your game library from a network drive sounds blasphemous. Contrary to common sense, turning your NAS into dedicated game storage is not only plausible, but it’s also a solid use case for your aging server – and it’s all thanks to the iSCSI protocol.

 Related
I tried storing all of my Steam games on a NAS - here's how it went

The experience wasn't all that bad thanks to the amazing iSCSI protocol

10

While working on this seemingly preposterous project last year, I tried storing my Steam games on an iSCSI-mapped network drive over a 2.5G connection. Despite the loading times taking a hit when I used an HDD iSCSI share, latency was never an issue, even in fast-paced titles where a single mistimed combo can result in a game over. A game storage server has become my go-to use case for old NAS units that are capable of leveraging faster SSDs and can handle at least 2.5 GbE speeds.

2 Test cool distributions

Better yet, replace the proprietary OS with a useful distro

Distro hopping can be a slippery slope to tread: one day you’re trying out a slightly different flavor of Debian, and the next day you’re ready to set up the most obscure distribution in the Linux ecosystem. Since my older NAS units can handle a VM or two, I often used them to virtualize different distributions before I eventually moved on to my Xeon workstation.

After making that monumental decision, I immediately wiped the proprietary OS off my outdated NAS and went with a bare-metal Proxmox installation. After all, NAS manufacturers tend to drop warranty support and updates for older rigs after a couple of years, so it made sense to replace the somewhat intuitive first-party OS with Proxmox and use the NAS as a secondary node for my PVE cluster. While we’re on the subject of home labs…

1 Self-hosting battlestation

From HASS to Frigate, the sky’s the limit!

Even if you’re not a fan of flashing a custom distribution on your pre-built NAS, most first-party operating systems let you run containers on your storage server. The best part? You don’t even need a top-of-the-line NAS with multiple CPU cores and tons of RAM to host services inside containers. Any old quad-core NAS should be able to run dozens of life-changing containers.

I’ve got a couple of surveillance cameras that store footage to my Frigate containers, while a fully-specc’d Nextcloud instance serves as my Office 365 alternative. If you’ve got as many random images, screenshots, and videos saved over the years as I do, you can use your NAS to run an Immich server. While a budget-friendly NAS may not be able to provide top-tier performance in transcoding workloads, it’s still a valid option for a Jellyfin-based media streaming machine. For folks who despise managing paperwork and official documents as much as I do, Paperless-ngx is hands-down the most underrated self-hosted service you can deploy on your NAS.

There’s still a lot you can do with your aging storage companion

If you’re still hungry for more projects you can build with your NAS, I’ve got some more suggestions. So long as your NAS doesn’t guzzle too much energy, you can arm it with a router OS such as pfSense, OPNsense, or OpenWRT and use it as a dedicated network firewall. Although I use a mini-PC for this purpose, an aging NAS can also double as a rock-solid Proxmox Backup Server if you have a PVE workstation in your home lab.

item.title: 6 things I learned from shrinking my keyboard progressively
item.itunes:title: 6 things I learned from shrinking my keyboard progressively
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
The world of mechanical keyboards is a wonderfully deep rabbit hole, filled with a myriad of switches, keycaps, and an ever-evolving landscape of experimental layouts created by hobbyists chasing the smallest improvement in comfort, size, or efficiency. For many, the journey starts with a full-size keyboard out of sheer familiarity when transitioning from membrane keyboards. If you're daring and curious, chances are you've tried using some of the popular compact layouts like a Ten keyless (TKL) to reclaim some desk space. But if you don't stop there and spend enough time lurking on r/mk, you eventually discover the vibrant niche of compact boards — usually those 75% layouts, and smaller.

I fell deep into the rabbit hole and gradually worked my way from a full-size keyboard to a TKL, then a 75%, 65%, 60%, 40%, eventually culminating in the Alpha by Pryrool — a 28-key board that just has the letters and two other keys. These diminutive input devices often spark curiosity and a fair bit of skepticism about their practicality for daily use. After all, how can you possibly be productive when you're "missing" so many keys?

It turns out that these compact layouts, ranging from the relatively spacious 75% and 65% boards down to the more extreme 40% or even 30% varieties, aren't just about aesthetics or saving a few square inches on your desk. They represent a fundamental rethinking of how we interact with our most-used tool. By stripping away dedicated keys and embracing layers, these boards seemingly counterintuitively unlock surprising gains in efficiency and comfort for those willing to adapt. My journey down this path of progressively smaller keyboards has been an enlightening one, and now I wouldn't encourage everyone to go down this road. Here's why.

 Related
Different keyboard sizes and layouts explained

Finding the right keyboard size for you can be easy, once you know where to start.

6 Small keyboards are more efficient

But only up to a point

Alpha by PyrooL with golden keycaps

One of the first and most profound realizations after switching to a 60% board was just how much more efficient a smaller keyboard can feel. Conventional keyboards, with their sprawling layouts, often require significant hand movement. Reaching for numbers, function keys, or even Backspace can break your typing rhythm. A compact keyboard, particularly a 40% layout, forces your fingers to stay anchored on or very near the home row. Suddenly, every essential character is just a quick hop away, either on the row above, below, or accessible via a quick layer toggle that requires holding down a second key. This dramatically reduces distracting movement for touch typists, especially for your pinkies and thumbs.

The streamlined and limited range of motion on a compact board feels more fluid and less taxing. Layers worked wonders for my typing speed and made long writing sessions far more comfortable. Adapting to accessing less-used keys via layers, much like pressing Shift for an uppercase letter, quickly becomes second nature.

Lodash 33

Lodash 33 with white MT3 profile keycaps

However, there’s a crucial caveat: the law of diminishing returns plays a role, too. While going from a full-size to a 60% or 40% board brought significant efficiency gains, shrinking the board past that point further started to feel less optimal for my workflow. I found that for me, a minimum of around 33 key strikes the perfect balance. For this reason, the Lodash 33 remained my daily driver even after I built the Alpha I mentioned earlier. Past this point, the cognitive load of remembering increasingly complex layer combinations for infrequent punctuations and symbols began to outweigh the typing speed benefits. Your experience may vary, and adept users may thrive on even smaller layouts. However, in my daily writing, that 33-key threshold was where peak efficiency met practical usability.

5 Your memory won't break a sweat

Even with four functions per key

Four basic layers set up on a standard 40% keyboard in green

As I've alluded to previously, programmable firmware like QMK (Quantum Mechanical Keyboard) and the integration of custom layers make smaller keyboards as usable as they are. Initially, the idea of having numerals, symbols, and navigation keys hidden behind layer activator keys can sound daunting, and it sure did to me at first when a friend lent me their Contra40 while I was using a TKL for work. "How could you possibly remember where everything is?" I remember asking. However, it all changed when I explored the sheer depth of customization detailed in the firmware documentation for QMK.

Once I started using layers on my TKL, I was pleasantly surprised to find that even with up to four functions per key (base, shift, and two additional custom layers), my brain adapted much faster than I anticipated. Excluding the standard "shifted" characters, I comfortably operate with four main layers. For instance, my home row keys become bottom row modifiers when I hold them down, and I find navigation arrows under IJKL when I hold down the spacebar with my left thumb. Accessing these layers doesn't always mean holding down a key either; features like layer toggles or One Shot Mods, where tapping a layer key activates it for just the subsequent keypress, add further convenience.

It becomes second nature surprisingly quickly, much like how we intuitively switch between alpha and symbol layers on our smartphone keyboards. For the first week or so, I kept a printed keymap of my layers on my desk for quick reference, and that was enough to build muscle memory as I learned to touch type on a smaller keyboard. The key is consistent placement and logical groupings. Soon enough, your fingers just know where to go, without conscious thought.

4 I was eventually underutilizing larger boards

The ghosts of keyboards past

The Keychron V1 Max is a great backup keyboard

An unexpected consequence of adapting to hyper-compact layouts became apparent when I switched back to a larger board while my smaller board awaited minor repairs. I keep a 75% Keychron V1 as a reliable and programmable backup. For consistency, I mapped all the keys shared with the smaller board to match in function, while other keys I was "missing" were left with the default mapping. I immediately found myself practically ignoring most of these extra keys that didn't exist on my compact daily. My fingers were so accustomed to the efficiency of my compact layout that the dedicated arrow cluster, the function row, and even some of the navigation keys on the 75% felt superfluous.

 Related
Keychron V1 Max review: A stellar typing experience at a reasonable price

You'll be hard-pressed to find a better-feeling keyboard at this price.

I've never been so thankful for my backup keyboard also being QMK-compatible, because if I had to use it with default mappings, it would have felt incredibly inefficient. The muscle memory for those compact, layer-driven actions had become deeply ingrained. It highlighted just how many keys on a traditional keyboard go unused or require awkward reaches once you've experienced a more streamlined system.

This isn't to say larger keyboards are inherently bad, but once you've tasted the hyper-optimized workflow of a tiny board, you start to view the "extra" keys on bigger boards as wasted potential and wasted space. The compact form factor rewired my brain, and it's hard to unlearn that, even when presented with more physical keys. You find yourself creating those familiar layers even when you have dedicated modifiers available. The biggest demerit here is that I now cannot use standard non-programmable keyboards without making a conscious effort, and I usually have to take my own keyboard anywhere I anticipate typing will be involved.

3 You may not even notice the space savings

It wasn't the primary objective, anyway

Even to an outsider, the most immediately obvious advantage of a 40% keyboard is its diminutive size and the extra desk space it would free up. If you're moving from a behemoth 100% keyboard to a 40% (not immediately, I hope), the reclaimed real estate is a game-changer, especially on smaller desks. More room for your mouse, a notebook, that to-do list, or just a cleaner aesthetic is undeniably pleasant.

However, as I went from a TKL (which is already fairly compact) to 60%, then 40%, and even smaller, I found that the incremental space saved wasn't the primary driver for me. Yes, a 33% board is tiny and incredibly portable, but the functional benefits of having every key within one unit of the home row became the dominant factor. The comfort and efficiency gains in typing were far more impactful than the extra inch or two of mousing room.

Unless you have a truly cramped workspace or frequently travel with your keyboard, the novelty of extreme compactness can wear off if the layout itself isn't making you more efficient. For most people, the space delta between, say, a 60% and a 40% board might be noticeable but not life-changing on an average-sized desk. The real win is workflow optimization, and space savings are a fortunate byproduct more than the objective for many who venture into sub-70% territory.

2 The untapped potential of your "other" thumb

Embrace the split spacebar

This is a hill I will die on: every keyboard should at least offer the option of a split spacebar. On traditional keyboards, one thumb, usually the dominant one, mashes on the spacebar, while the other thumb does… Well, not much. It sits there as an idle appendage that occasionally assists with spacebar duty. Once I started using keyboards with split spacebars, it was a revelation.

Giving that second thumb a job, or even splitting the duties of the primary spacebar into smaller, more manageable keys, unlocks a new dimension of ergonomic efficiency. On my compact boards, one key might be assigned to space while the other can be a crucial second key that also requires frequent access. I have mine mapped to Enter, but I've also seen people access Backspace or Delete with it. This distributes the workload more evenly across both hands and keeps your fingers closer to the home row by eliminating the need to reach for distant keys with the weaker fingers of either hand.

The improvement in comfort and speed by assigning common actions to a non-dominant thumb is remarkable. You reduce awkward stretches and can maintain a more consistent typing rhythm. A split spacebar can be a game-changer even on larger keyboards, allowing for custom configurations that significantly enhance your workflow. It’s such a simple yet profound ergonomic improvement that it’s baffling why it’s not more common outside the custom mechanical keyboard scene.

1 Smaller isn't always better for everything

And certainly not for everyone

Despite my enthusiasm, it's crucial to acknowledge that ultra-compact keyboards aren't a one-size-fits-all solution. There are definite scenarios where their minimalist nature becomes a hindrance rather than a help. Even if you negate related peripherals like macropads, many gamers rely on quick, unambiguous access to a wide array of keys, including the number row, function keys, and sometimes even macros. While you can game on a 40% keyboard using layers, it often introduces an unwanted layer of complexity that can be detrimental in demanding situations.

Similarly, for tasks involving heavy numerical input — think data entry, accounting, or extensive use of spreadsheets — the absence of a dedicated number pad on most compact layouts is a significant drawback. I would be lying if I said I didn't miss a dedicated numpad in CAD software where entering dimensions is standard fare. Accessing numbers via a layer is perfectly fine for occasional use, but it's considerably slower and more error-prone than a physical numpad for high-volume input. While some compact layouts cleverly integrate a numpad into a layer on ortholinear keyboards, it rarely matches the raw speed and convenience of dedicated hardware for specialized tasks.

The beauty of these layouts often lies in their customization for text-heavy tasks like writing and coding, where chorded actions and layers can shine. However, for applications that demand many discrete keys accessible without modifiers, a larger keyboard often remains the more practical choice. It’s about choosing the right tool for the job, and sometimes, that tool needs to have more keys physically present.

A niche worth exploring, carefully

My journey of trying out progressively smaller keyboards until I reached a breaking point sure isn't one I regret. It reshaped my understanding of typing efficiency and comfort, proving that less can indeed be more. However, it's also taught me that these compact wonders aren't for everyone or every task. The learning curve is real, and the mental rewiring required might not be worth it for many users.

Perhaps it's a good thing that many of these truly smaller boards often come as DIY kits, existing as a niche within the mechanical keyboard niche. That said, these boards offer personalization that's hard to find in off-the-shelf products. It also means you're making a conscious investment in a tool for your trade. If you're curious to go down this exploratory path too, I'd wholeheartedly recommend attending local mechanical keyboard community meetups if possible, where you can often try out these unique layouts before committing your time and money to building one and learning to use it.
item.description:
The world of mechanical keyboards is a wonderfully deep rabbit hole, filled with a myriad of switches, keycaps, and an ever-evolving landscape of experimental layouts created by hobbyists chasing the smallest improvement in comfort, size, or efficiency. For many, the journey starts with a full-size keyboard out of sheer familiarity when transitioning from membrane keyboards. If you're daring and curious, chances are you've tried using some of the popular compact layouts like a Ten keyless (TKL) to reclaim some desk space. But if you don't stop there and spend enough time lurking on r/mk, you eventually discover the vibrant niche of compact boards — usually those 75% layouts, and smaller.

I fell deep into the rabbit hole and gradually worked my way from a full-size keyboard to a TKL, then a 75%, 65%, 60%, 40%, eventually culminating in the Alpha by Pryrool — a 28-key board that just has the letters and two other keys. These diminutive input devices often spark curiosity and a fair bit of skepticism about their practicality for daily use. After all, how can you possibly be productive when you're "missing" so many keys?

It turns out that these compact layouts, ranging from the relatively spacious 75% and 65% boards down to the more extreme 40% or even 30% varieties, aren't just about aesthetics or saving a few square inches on your desk. They represent a fundamental rethinking of how we interact with our most-used tool. By stripping away dedicated keys and embracing layers, these boards seemingly counterintuitively unlock surprising gains in efficiency and comfort for those willing to adapt. My journey down this path of progressively smaller keyboards has been an enlightening one, and now I wouldn't encourage everyone to go down this road. Here's why.

 Related
Different keyboard sizes and layouts explained

Finding the right keyboard size for you can be easy, once you know where to start.

6 Small keyboards are more efficient

But only up to a point

Alpha by PyrooL with golden keycaps

One of the first and most profound realizations after switching to a 60% board was just how much more efficient a smaller keyboard can feel. Conventional keyboards, with their sprawling layouts, often require significant hand movement. Reaching for numbers, function keys, or even Backspace can break your typing rhythm. A compact keyboard, particularly a 40% layout, forces your fingers to stay anchored on or very near the home row. Suddenly, every essential character is just a quick hop away, either on the row above, below, or accessible via a quick layer toggle that requires holding down a second key. This dramatically reduces distracting movement for touch typists, especially for your pinkies and thumbs.

The streamlined and limited range of motion on a compact board feels more fluid and less taxing. Layers worked wonders for my typing speed and made long writing sessions far more comfortable. Adapting to accessing less-used keys via layers, much like pressing Shift for an uppercase letter, quickly becomes second nature.

Lodash 33

Lodash 33 with white MT3 profile keycaps

However, there’s a crucial caveat: the law of diminishing returns plays a role, too. While going from a full-size to a 60% or 40% board brought significant efficiency gains, shrinking the board past that point further started to feel less optimal for my workflow. I found that for me, a minimum of around 33 key strikes the perfect balance. For this reason, the Lodash 33 remained my daily driver even after I built the Alpha I mentioned earlier. Past this point, the cognitive load of remembering increasingly complex layer combinations for infrequent punctuations and symbols began to outweigh the typing speed benefits. Your experience may vary, and adept users may thrive on even smaller layouts. However, in my daily writing, that 33-key threshold was where peak efficiency met practical usability.

5 Your memory won't break a sweat

Even with four functions per key

Four basic layers set up on a standard 40% keyboard in green

As I've alluded to previously, programmable firmware like QMK (Quantum Mechanical Keyboard) and the integration of custom layers make smaller keyboards as usable as they are. Initially, the idea of having numerals, symbols, and navigation keys hidden behind layer activator keys can sound daunting, and it sure did to me at first when a friend lent me their Contra40 while I was using a TKL for work. "How could you possibly remember where everything is?" I remember asking. However, it all changed when I explored the sheer depth of customization detailed in the firmware documentation for QMK.

Once I started using layers on my TKL, I was pleasantly surprised to find that even with up to four functions per key (base, shift, and two additional custom layers), my brain adapted much faster than I anticipated. Excluding the standard "shifted" characters, I comfortably operate with four main layers. For instance, my home row keys become bottom row modifiers when I hold them down, and I find navigation arrows under IJKL when I hold down the spacebar with my left thumb. Accessing these layers doesn't always mean holding down a key either; features like layer toggles or One Shot Mods, where tapping a layer key activates it for just the subsequent keypress, add further convenience.

It becomes second nature surprisingly quickly, much like how we intuitively switch between alpha and symbol layers on our smartphone keyboards. For the first week or so, I kept a printed keymap of my layers on my desk for quick reference, and that was enough to build muscle memory as I learned to touch type on a smaller keyboard. The key is consistent placement and logical groupings. Soon enough, your fingers just know where to go, without conscious thought.

4 I was eventually underutilizing larger boards

The ghosts of keyboards past

The Keychron V1 Max is a great backup keyboard

An unexpected consequence of adapting to hyper-compact layouts became apparent when I switched back to a larger board while my smaller board awaited minor repairs. I keep a 75% Keychron V1 as a reliable and programmable backup. For consistency, I mapped all the keys shared with the smaller board to match in function, while other keys I was "missing" were left with the default mapping. I immediately found myself practically ignoring most of these extra keys that didn't exist on my compact daily. My fingers were so accustomed to the efficiency of my compact layout that the dedicated arrow cluster, the function row, and even some of the navigation keys on the 75% felt superfluous.

 Related
Keychron V1 Max review: A stellar typing experience at a reasonable price

You'll be hard-pressed to find a better-feeling keyboard at this price.

I've never been so thankful for my backup keyboard also being QMK-compatible, because if I had to use it with default mappings, it would have felt incredibly inefficient. The muscle memory for those compact, layer-driven actions had become deeply ingrained. It highlighted just how many keys on a traditional keyboard go unused or require awkward reaches once you've experienced a more streamlined system.

This isn't to say larger keyboards are inherently bad, but once you've tasted the hyper-optimized workflow of a tiny board, you start to view the "extra" keys on bigger boards as wasted potential and wasted space. The compact form factor rewired my brain, and it's hard to unlearn that, even when presented with more physical keys. You find yourself creating those familiar layers even when you have dedicated modifiers available. The biggest demerit here is that I now cannot use standard non-programmable keyboards without making a conscious effort, and I usually have to take my own keyboard anywhere I anticipate typing will be involved.

3 You may not even notice the space savings

It wasn't the primary objective, anyway

Even to an outsider, the most immediately obvious advantage of a 40% keyboard is its diminutive size and the extra desk space it would free up. If you're moving from a behemoth 100% keyboard to a 40% (not immediately, I hope), the reclaimed real estate is a game-changer, especially on smaller desks. More room for your mouse, a notebook, that to-do list, or just a cleaner aesthetic is undeniably pleasant.

However, as I went from a TKL (which is already fairly compact) to 60%, then 40%, and even smaller, I found that the incremental space saved wasn't the primary driver for me. Yes, a 33% board is tiny and incredibly portable, but the functional benefits of having every key within one unit of the home row became the dominant factor. The comfort and efficiency gains in typing were far more impactful than the extra inch or two of mousing room.

Unless you have a truly cramped workspace or frequently travel with your keyboard, the novelty of extreme compactness can wear off if the layout itself isn't making you more efficient. For most people, the space delta between, say, a 60% and a 40% board might be noticeable but not life-changing on an average-sized desk. The real win is workflow optimization, and space savings are a fortunate byproduct more than the objective for many who venture into sub-70% territory.

2 The untapped potential of your "other" thumb

Embrace the split spacebar

This is a hill I will die on: every keyboard should at least offer the option of a split spacebar. On traditional keyboards, one thumb, usually the dominant one, mashes on the spacebar, while the other thumb does… Well, not much. It sits there as an idle appendage that occasionally assists with spacebar duty. Once I started using keyboards with split spacebars, it was a revelation.

Giving that second thumb a job, or even splitting the duties of the primary spacebar into smaller, more manageable keys, unlocks a new dimension of ergonomic efficiency. On my compact boards, one key might be assigned to space while the other can be a crucial second key that also requires frequent access. I have mine mapped to Enter, but I've also seen people access Backspace or Delete with it. This distributes the workload more evenly across both hands and keeps your fingers closer to the home row by eliminating the need to reach for distant keys with the weaker fingers of either hand.

The improvement in comfort and speed by assigning common actions to a non-dominant thumb is remarkable. You reduce awkward stretches and can maintain a more consistent typing rhythm. A split spacebar can be a game-changer even on larger keyboards, allowing for custom configurations that significantly enhance your workflow. It’s such a simple yet profound ergonomic improvement that it’s baffling why it’s not more common outside the custom mechanical keyboard scene.

1 Smaller isn't always better for everything

And certainly not for everyone

Despite my enthusiasm, it's crucial to acknowledge that ultra-compact keyboards aren't a one-size-fits-all solution. There are definite scenarios where their minimalist nature becomes a hindrance rather than a help. Even if you negate related peripherals like macropads, many gamers rely on quick, unambiguous access to a wide array of keys, including the number row, function keys, and sometimes even macros. While you can game on a 40% keyboard using layers, it often introduces an unwanted layer of complexity that can be detrimental in demanding situations.

Similarly, for tasks involving heavy numerical input — think data entry, accounting, or extensive use of spreadsheets — the absence of a dedicated number pad on most compact layouts is a significant drawback. I would be lying if I said I didn't miss a dedicated numpad in CAD software where entering dimensions is standard fare. Accessing numbers via a layer is perfectly fine for occasional use, but it's considerably slower and more error-prone than a physical numpad for high-volume input. While some compact layouts cleverly integrate a numpad into a layer on ortholinear keyboards, it rarely matches the raw speed and convenience of dedicated hardware for specialized tasks.

The beauty of these layouts often lies in their customization for text-heavy tasks like writing and coding, where chorded actions and layers can shine. However, for applications that demand many discrete keys accessible without modifiers, a larger keyboard often remains the more practical choice. It’s about choosing the right tool for the job, and sometimes, that tool needs to have more keys physically present.

A niche worth exploring, carefully

My journey of trying out progressively smaller keyboards until I reached a breaking point sure isn't one I regret. It reshaped my understanding of typing efficiency and comfort, proving that less can indeed be more. However, it's also taught me that these compact wonders aren't for everyone or every task. The learning curve is real, and the mental rewiring required might not be worth it for many users.

Perhaps it's a good thing that many of these truly smaller boards often come as DIY kits, existing as a niche within the mechanical keyboard niche. That said, these boards offer personalization that's hard to find in off-the-shelf products. It also means you're making a conscious investment in a tool for your trade. If you're curious to go down this exploratory path too, I'd wholeheartedly recommend attending local mechanical keyboard community meetups if possible, where you can often try out these unique layouts before committing your time and money to building one and learning to use it.

item.title: Gold-plated HDMI cables are a scam
item.itunes:title: Gold-plated HDMI cables are a scam
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
You've probably come across meaningless branding terms in the PC hardware space. Manufacturers do their best to sell common features as game-changing innovations to unsuspecting users. A similar scam is the gold-plated HDMI cable, marketed as being somehow superior to regular cables in terms of performance and durability. While gold does have anti-tarnish properties, even that doesn't matter to most users in indoor situations. The gold-plated HDMI cable is simply a way to oversell the usefulness of the metal to the majority of users.

 Related
6 “gaming” features on motherboards that are just marketing

These gaming features on your gaming motherboard might not be doing anything at all

5

3 Gold doesn't improve durability for the average user

Your device isn't enduring the outdoors

It's true that gold is resistant to oxidation or corrosion, unlike other metals like copper or silver. This tarnish resistance, however, doesn't matter to the average user who's plugging in an HDMI cable into their TV or PC. Your living room or computer room isn't subject to harsh weather or chemicals, so your HDMI cables will endure sustained use even without gold-plated connectors.

The strength of an HDMI cable is dependent on the materials used and features like anti-bending connectors, and even that comes into play only if you're removing and plugging the cables hundreds of times a year. The corrosion resistance of gold-plated connectors is valuable in outdoor or industrial use, where high humidity or chemicals can damage regular connectors. So, you can safely ignore higher-priced HDMI cables that aggressively market gold-plated connectors.

 Related
The great RAM doubler hoax of the 90s: How SoftRAM scammed over 650,000 PC users

Syncronys claimed it doubled your computer’s RAM—but it did absolutely nothing. Here’s the story behind one of the biggest tech scams of the 90s.

1

2 The gold plating does nothing to improve performance

Focus on the HDMI standard

Many product pages for HDMI cables indirectly suggest that gold-plated connectors improve the performance of your streaming experience. The HDMI standard and materials of the cable determine the bandwidth and, thereby, the performance you get out of it. Whether or not your connectors are plated with gold doesn't have any effect on the quality of the signal.

Irrespective of whether you need to transmit a 4K@60Hz, 4K@120Hz, or 8K@30Hz signal, you need to focus on the actual bandwidth of the cable (in Gbps). The design and treatment of the cable will not help you "improve" your gaming or media streaming experience.

 Related
6 recent PC hardware scandals that still seem unbelievable

Some PC hardware scandals are tough to forget. These are some of the most recent and shocking ones on record.

2

1 Gold doesn't magically improve the signal strength

Who is using lengthy cables?

Another claim made by HDMI cable manufacturers goes something like this: "Gold-plated interface, signal transmission stable" or "tinplate metal shielding and gold-plated connectors to reduce interference and ensure stable signal transmission." These statements try to convince you that gold-plated connectors will ensure a "stable" signal and prevent any dropouts.

While this might be valuable in scenarios where cables measuring hundreds of feet need to be routed, who is doing that for their TV or gaming PC? Standard HDMI cables don't experience signal degradation even up to 50 feet, which is way more than what most people use at home. Your HDMI cable is probably 3 to 6 feet in length, which is far from even the conservative 15 feet limit most people suggest. So, forget about signal loss or degradation when buying standard-length HDMI cables.

 Related
Stop it already: 6 PC hardware trends that need to go away

As Adam Jensen would have said: "I never asked for this"

7

All that glitters is not gold

Gold-plated HDMI connectors aren't remotely useful for the average person using a PC, laptop, or TV. Any durability or signal stability benefits marketed by manufacturers aren't relevant to most indoor setups. The corrosion resistance of gold comes into play only in industrial or outdoor scenarios, where clean contact surfaces need to be maintained. Your home HDMI cable isn't getting oxidized or corroded, so stop falling for the gold-plated cable scam.

It's true that gold is resistant to oxidation or corrosion, unlike other metals like copper or silver. This tarnish resistance, however, doesn't matter to the average user who's plugging in an HDMI cable into their TV or PC. Your living room or computer room isn't subject to harsh weather or chemicals, so your HDMI cables will endure sustained use even without gold-plated connectors.

The strength of an HDMI cable is dependent on the materials used and features like anti-bending connectors, and even that comes into play only if you're removing and plugging the cables hundreds of times a year. The corrosion resistance of gold-plated connectors is valuable in outdoor or industrial use, where high humidity or chemicals can damage regular connectors. So, you can safely ignore higher-priced HDMI cables that aggressively market gold-plated connectors.

 Related
The great RAM doubler hoax of the 90s: How SoftRAM scammed over 650,000 PC users

Syncronys claimed it doubled your computer’s RAM—but it did absolutely nothing. Here’s the story behind one of the biggest tech scams of the 90s.

1

2 The gold plating does nothing to improve performance

Focus on the HDMI standard

Many product pages for HDMI cables indirectly suggest that gold-plated connectors improve the performance of your streaming experience. The HDMI standard and materials of the cable determine the bandwidth and, thereby, the performance you get out of it. Whether or not your connectors are plated with gold doesn't have any effect on the quality of the signal.

Irrespective of whether you need to transmit a 4K@60Hz, 4K@120Hz, or 8K@30Hz signal, you need to focus on the actual bandwidth of the cable (in Gbps). The design and treatment of the cable will not help you "improve" your gaming or media streaming experience.

 Related
6 recent PC hardware scandals that still seem unbelievable

Some PC hardware scandals are tough to forget. These are some of the most recent and shocking ones on record.

2

1 Gold doesn't magically improve the signal strength

Who is using lengthy cables?

Another claim made by HDMI cable manufacturers goes something like this: "Gold-plated interface, signal transmission stable" or "tinplate metal shielding and gold-plated connectors to reduce interference and ensure stable signal transmission." These statements try to convince you that gold-plated connectors will ensure a "stable" signal and prevent any dropouts.

While this might be valuable in scenarios where cables measuring hundreds of feet need to be routed, who is doing that for their TV or gaming PC? Standard HDMI cables don't experience signal degradation even up to 50 feet, which is way more than what most people use at home. Your HDMI cable is probably 3 to 6 feet in length, which is far from even the conservative 15 feet limit most people suggest. So, forget about signal loss or degradation when buying standard-length HDMI cables.

 Related
Stop it already: 6 PC hardware trends that need to go away

As Adam Jensen would have said: "I never asked for this"

7

All that glitters is not gold

Gold-plated HDMI connectors aren't remotely useful for the average person using a PC, laptop, or TV. Any durability or signal stability benefits marketed by manufacturers aren't relevant to most indoor setups. The corrosion resistance of gold comes into play only in industrial or outdoor scenarios, where clean contact surfaces need to be maintained. Your home HDMI cable isn't getting oxidized or corroded, so stop falling for the gold-plated cable scam.
item.content:encoded:
You've probably come across meaningless branding terms in the PC hardware space. Manufacturers do their best to sell common features as game-changing innovations to unsuspecting users. A similar scam is the gold-plated HDMI cable, marketed as being somehow superior to regular cables in terms of performance and durability. While gold does have anti-tarnish properties, even that doesn't matter to most users in indoor situations. The gold-plated HDMI cable is simply a way to oversell the usefulness of the metal to the majority of users.

 Related
6 “gaming” features on motherboards that are just marketing

These gaming features on your gaming motherboard might not be doing anything at all

5

3 Gold doesn't improve durability for the average user

Your device isn't enduring the outdoors

It's true that gold is resistant to oxidation or corrosion, unlike other metals like copper or silver. This tarnish resistance, however, doesn't matter to the average user who's plugging in an HDMI cable into their TV or PC. Your living room or computer room isn't subject to harsh weather or chemicals, so your HDMI cables will endure sustained use even without gold-plated connectors.

The strength of an HDMI cable is dependent on the materials used and features like anti-bending connectors, and even that comes into play only if you're removing and plugging the cables hundreds of times a year. The corrosion resistance of gold-plated connectors is valuable in outdoor or industrial use, where high humidity or chemicals can damage regular connectors. item.title: These are the Switch 2 games I'm playing on day one
item.itunes:title: These are the Switch 2 games I'm playing on day one
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
The Nintendo Switch 2 has finally arrived, and after the first day with mine, I'm quite happy with the new console. Not only does much of the hardware feel upgraded, the Joy-Cons are better, the Switch is a bit bigger, and the Pro Controller has a great new feel to it. All of that is great, but what I care most about are the games. Mario Kart World was an obvious first pick to boot up, since it came packed in with my console, but on top of that I checked out a few other games. None of those other games are new, technically, but the Switch 2 enhancements have made some games feel new.

 Related
6 reasons I can't wait for the Switch 2 — not even including the new games

A brand new generation of Nintendo is almost upon us

2

5 Mario Kart World

It's been a long time since we got a new Mario Kart

Mario Kart World is the launch game for the Nintendo Switch, coming in the more expensive bundle version of the console. It's also Nintendo's first $80 game, and while I'm not yet ready to say if it's worth the price, it is quite good so far. I've played a handful of regular cups, some on 50CC, since I will need to if I want three-star gold on all the cups, and one a 150CC, just to see how it feels at top speed. While the openness of some tracks is throwing me off, and I'm not sure how I feel about the large number of point-to-point races. That said, the racing feels great, and if you are worried it feels too much like Mario Kart 8, there are tons of small gameplay additions, like rail grinding, wall driving, and charged jumps, that make it feel quite different once you understand it. The character roster is nuts too, although I do think the novelty of having that many characters will wear off eventually.

7 Images
7 Images
Mario Kart World
Racing
Open-World
Systems
Released
June 5, 2025

WHERE TO PLAY

DIGITAL
Powered by

4 The Legend of Zelda: Tears of the Kingdom

Buttery smooth

While I want to check out both Zelda upgrades at some point, I figured I should pick just one for launch day. I went with Tears of the Kingdom, where I booted up my old save from my second playthrough, which I left off in the Fire Temple. While I don't necessarily feel like I could notice a huge boost in resolution and visual quality, although being in the underground lava area probably impacted that, the improved technical aspects were obvious right away. Not only does it run at 60 FPS, it does so consistently. I don't remember which areas of Tears of the Kingdom ran poorly before to really put it to the test, but playing through part of the dungeon I never experienced any dropped frames or choppiness. Breath of the Wild will likely feel like a bigger upgrade when the time comes, since I haven't played that game in several years, but I was immediately impress with how Tears of the Kingdom runs. While I might not do another complete playthrough, I will spend more time with it in the future.

The Legend of Zelda: Tears of the Kingdom
Adventure
Systems
Released
May 12, 2023

WHERE TO PLAY

DIGITAL
PHYSICAL
Powered by

3 Pokémon Scarlet

Both a drastic and disappointing upgrade

Pokémon Scarlet and Violet did not receive paid upgrades like the Zelda games did, but they did receive free updates to boost performance. The performance has been boosted, but it's also exposed some of the game's shortcomings in really obvious ways. Having beaten the game and the DLCs previously, I wiped my save and started fresh. Pokémon Scarlet runs at a stable 60 FPS, which is jarring to look at considering how poorly the games ran on the original Switch. It does run at a higher resolution, but this improved performance has made some of the bad textures and lackluster art-style look even worse now. It's a bit of a bummer, because Pokémon Scarlet and Violet are some of the best designed games in the series in terms of how the game flows, but it really could have used some extra work. That said, being able to play it at 60 FPS is tempting me to play it again, just so I can see the game it was meant to be.

Powered by

2 Nintendo Switch 2 Welcome Tour

I had to know

While most people will likely dodge Nintendo's $10 Switch 2 tutorial, Nintendo Switch 2 Welcome Tour, I found it too intriguing to leave it alone. While I only scratched the surface of it, I liked what I played enough to come back and see it through, although I will give a warning. There are minigames here that require additional accessories, specifically a Pro Controller 2 and a USB Camera. While you can use any camera that is USB-C, the Pro Controller costs nearly $100. You also technically need a 4K display, although that's far more common than the extra accessories. There are very few minigames that require these, but I think people should know that. All of that said, the minigames are fun and feel like WarioWare in some ways. It's better than I expected, even if I'm still not sure people should have to pay for it.

Nintendo Switch 2 Welcome Tour
Mini-Games
Systems
Released
June 5, 2025
Developer(s)
Nintendo
Publisher(s)
Nintendo
Powered by

1 SoulCaliber II - GameCube NSO

The emulation seems solid

Lastly, I booted up SoulCalibur II on the GameCube Nintendo Switch Online, which also launched with The Legend of Zelda: Wind Waker and F-Zero GX. While I am more interested in playing the other two games, I had a limited amount of time and the fighting game seemed like the quickest thing to jump into. I haven't played this one before, but I have played a decent amount of SoulCalibur VI, although I'm not great at fighting games. I played a few rounds here and the game feels great to play, the latency is extremely low, and it looks pretty good. I played a few rounds using the NSO GameCube controller which Nintendo sells on its website. This wireless controller works for other Switch 2 games, but it feels perfect for these GameCube games. The shoulder buttons feel great on the new controller, and I had fun messing around in SoulCalibur II.

6 Images
6 Images
Soulcalibur II
Fighting
Action
Systems
Released
July 5, 2002

WHERE TO PLAY

PHYSICAL
Powered by

A buffet of new and improved experiences

While most of my attention will move to Mario Kart World for the next couple of weeks, I'm excited about my Nintendo Switch 2. While others might not find joy in old games running better, I do, and seeing Tears of the Kingdom and Pokémon Scarlet running so much better on this console has me excited for new games. The last year or so, my Switch has gone untouched, since Nintendo has had fewer releases and third-party games just ran too poorly on it, but now I feel like I can pick up any new game on the Switch 2 and have a great time.

Nintendo Switch 2
4K Capability
Yes
4K Capabilities
4K 60Hz (TV mode only)
$450 at Best Buy $449 at Walmart $450 at Target $450 at GameStop
